title: A Reference Architecture
concept: A full system diagram: host app, MCP servers, vector store, reranker, and grounded generation working together.

exercises.prompt: In the previous lesson, we learned about 'Context Engineering Across the Stack.' What does this practice primarily focus on?

options.text: Buying more cloud storage to save raw files.
Prioritizing and trimming tools, history, and retrieved chunks to fit LLM limits.
Training the LLM on a larger dataset.

exercises.explain: Managing your context budget requires prioritizing and trimming less important information (like long chat histories or low-score search chunks) to keep the most relevant data within the LLM's limit.

cards.title: Putting the Pieces Together
cards.body: We have learned about vector databases, live tools, and context budgets. Now, let's look at the complete master blueprint where all these components connect to form an enterprise-grade AI system.

cards.title: What is a Reference Architecture?
cards.body: A Reference Architecture for agentic RAG is a blueprint that maps out how the host application, vector databases, rerankers, and Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers work in harmony. It ensures that the LLM receives both deeply researched historical context and live, action-oriented tool data.

cards.title: The Grand Kitchen Orchestra
cards.body: Think of the system like a high-end restaurant kitchen. The Host App is the head chef orchestrating the order, the MCP Servers are the pantry prep cooks providing fresh ingredients, and the Vector Store is the library of classic recipes. The Reranker is the sous chef who selects only the absolute best recipe matches to present to the Executive Chef (the LLM) to plate the final dish.

cards.title: Support Agent in Action
cards.body: Imagine building a customer support assistant. The Host App grabs past tickets from a Vector Store, runs them through a Reranker to keep only the 3 most relevant cases, uses an MCP database server to look up the customer's active subscription, and hands all of this filtered data to the LLM to write a perfectly informed email.

cards.title: More Parts, Better Performance
cards.body: You might think that putting more components in the architecture makes the system slower and less reliable. In truth, using a dedicated Reranker and structured MCP servers actually reduces LLM processing time and costs by filtering out noisy, irrelevant data before it ever reaches the LLM.

cards.title: Knowledge vs. Action
cards.body: Let's compare the role of the Vector Store (history) against the MCP Server (live action) in the overall architecture.

left.text: Stores thousands of pages of static manuals, documentation, and past conversation logs.

right.text: Executes real-time actions like looking up a database or fetching current weather.

exercises.prompt: Which component acts as the central coordinator in our reference architecture?

options.text: The Vector Store, because it is the only component that can run code.
The Host App, which coordinates the data flow between all components.
The Reranker, because it generates the final text response.
The MCP Server, which directly replaces the LLM.

exercises.explain: The Host App acts as the coordinator or central brain of the architecture, managing the flow of data between the user, the database, the tools, and the LLM.

exercises.prompt: True or False: In a standard reference architecture, the MCP server and the Vector Store are the exact same component.
exercises.explain: MCP servers handle external tool execution and live data retrieval, while vector stores handle semantic search over static historical documents. They serve different purposes.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing component in this system flow.
exercises.sentence: After the vector store retrieves raw chunks, the reranker selects the most relevant ones.
exercises.explain: The reranker acts as a precision filter between raw retrieval and the LLM, ensuring we don't waste our context budget.

exercises.prompt: Identify which description of system architecture components contains AI-generated nonsense.

options.text: The host app queries the vector store, reranks the hits, calls an MCP server for user status, and sends the context to the LLM.
The vector store executes MCP commands to compile code, while the reranker translates natural language into database tables for the LLM.
A user query goes to the database, gets matched by similarity, and the top results are sent straight to the model.

exercises.explain: Option B shows an AI attempting to explain a reference architecture flow but hallucinates that the Vector Store executes tools and the Reranker writes code. Option A is a standard description of agentic RAG, and Option C describes classic RAG.

exercises.prompt: Trace a user's request through a complete reference architecture. What is the correct order of operations to produce a grounded, real-time response?

options.text: Host App -> fetches chunks -> Reranker filters -> MCP tools query live data -> LLM generates grounded answer.
Vector Store -> bypasses Host App -> directly executes MCP code -> updates LLM weights.
MCP Server -> updates Vector Store -> bypasses Reranker -> tells LLM to stop generating.
Reranker -> replaces the Host App -> executes local bash commands directly.

exercises.explain: A full reference architecture is not just a chain; it is an active loop. The Host App receives a query, fetches raw chunks from the Vector Store, refines them using the Reranker, and simultaneously queries MCP tools for live status before sending the consolidated prompt to the LLM to generate a grounded, accurate response.
